item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I want to upvote this but I can't get past the procedure thing.

Why are they deafening people who are affected? There's no indication that it would help in any way.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Holy fucking hell I love this SCP. This is incredible. It's so bizarre, the disconnected things between the effect and origin, the implication that this POSSIBLY happening retroactively, also how it was DISCOVERED BY THE MEDICAL COMMUNITY?!

This is incredible. Wow. Top 3 SCPs, wow. DisquietRiot if you're still around this is great.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: You never say exactly why becoming deaf after being affected "cures" you, and I can not stand that kind of oversight. Downvoted

item.title: Name Redaction
item.content:encoded:
Dr. Nadeem █████████ is unnecessary. The issue is, the article is still publicly available, so why redact? Anyone can look up the article by title or journal with publication date and see who wrote it. And even if the Foundation erased/destroyed all traces of the article, you can't feasibly give amnesiacs to every clinician who diagnoses this complex. So the black boxing honestly comes across as you being too lazy to find a last name for this researcher.

I try to vote based on the quality and merit of the individual scip entry without comparing it to others or not liking it based on the genre used. I felt your article uses body horror as a gimmick instead of being an awesome article that just so happens to be about body horror. I would downvote if it was any other genre but am instead novoting just in case I am being prejudicial because of my dislike of body horror.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: So, I'm guessing the black-boxed names are "Isaac Brock" and "Clap Your Hands Say Yeah" respectively?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is an incredibly silly piece of feedback. It's a total non-issue.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: But it's also not scary. You can't rely on "how many other things are there out there like this" without some special effort, because it's done so much. It's just background noise, unless you do something like "convince the reader that an everyday object/animal/etc is a Scary Thing" or something like that, which is off-point for this concept.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
It's got the core of a good SCP, but the bulk of the SCP is dull and unnecessary. The dreams are really good, but the rest… not so much. I'm not even sure why you have the musical element at all, since you don't do anything interesting with it that I can see.

Therefore, -1. Hoping that you'll work on it further.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The albums that affect 100% of people bother me, because of their popularity. Even if it only affects certain printings, that's still a *lot* of people with chunks of memory and/or hearing mysteriously missing. When they all go to the doctor wondering why they suddenly went deaf and find that their eardrums had been punctured, I'd think *someone* would notice. Also, how does puncturing their eardrums help after the fact anyway? As an extreme preventative measure, sure, but as a cure it makes no sense. I guess it lets the bad juju leak out?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: That was indeed a typo, thank you for fixing it!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Lavinia is not the doctor's name, it's just what he decided to temporarily call the disorder and the name sort of stuck. It's a reference to Shakespeare's Titus Andronicus.

Like how the Oedipus Complex was not discovered by a dude named Oedipus.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
They track them down by investigating any reports of the Livania Complex (or its symptoms) in "the wild." They also probably randomly test a huge number of albums, looking for other songs/artists/albums that trigger those finger-licking good dreams. If this sounds reactive (as opposed to proactive) and tedious, well… it kinda is. This is the MTF's entire job, and their number one research priority is trying to find better ways of predicting this SCP's spread.

If this was a single album that made you dream repetitively about eating your hands, then it would be pretty boring. Just lock it in a box and be done with it. The only reason this is spooky at all is because it's borderline impossible to predict, and has basically been in containment breach for over a decade.

Basically your criticism of "how do they even find these instances" are valid, and the Foundation is asking itself the very same thing. The fact that they authorized their agents to surgically deafening civilians is a pretty good indicator they're getting desperate.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I was going to upvote until I saw this:

Listeners exposed to SCP-2142 will suffer from an anomalous sleep disorder dubbed the "Lavinia Complex" by its discoverer, Dr. Nadeem █████████

Why is the last name redacted when it is obvious from the name of the complex what the last name is?

Neutral no-vote until that is fixed/addressed.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like a bit of body horror and it's well written, but it just didn't grab me. Unless I just missed them completely, there are no narrative threads to tug on. I wondered if something would happen with the doctor but that petered out pretty quick. No little hints at the cause or why fingers specifically. Nothing to hint at anything deeper than just what's in the SCP.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The how is intended to be part of the horror here

But that's the problem. I don't find that particular mystery horrifying, I find it confusing. Having an unknown number still out there is well and good, but unless the Foundation literally just stumbled on every copy independently (which seriously strains credulity), then there has to be some method by which they can track these down.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
+1. However, one minor nitpick:

Even if the containment procedures are very mundane/simple, I'm not sure that "no special containment procedures" is an accurate descriptor. If they get stored in a particular storage area, that is containment procedures.

Fixed a typo, also (amnetics -> amnestics; just wanted to mention here in case that was on purpose?)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The how is intended to be part of the horror here. They have eighteen hundred right now, but they're not sure how many exist out there. They also straight up admit in the containment procedures that they don't know how this thing propagates or how it selects the songs it affects. This uncertainty is hinted at a little bit (the guy who gets designated a PoI, only to get re-designated a PoI again later. They're grasping at straws trying to figure out what makes this thing tick.)

So, yeah. New instances of this thing are really hard to discover. But they're still out there, waiting in the bargain bin of a record store somewhere.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The body horror is nice, especially since it's just dreams and not an actual waking compulsion. And while the delivery method is novel, I'm confused by how the Foundation identified these particular songs/albums are the delivery method. You even state that only specific printings of them cause it, rather than every version ever of the song. For instance, how in the world did they discover that The Beatles caused it, but only that particular song, and only on that particular printing, and only sometimes? No vote for now.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This didnt strike home for me. It seems a little too hit and miss; you may or may not dream about eating your hands, you may or may not wake up before eating your hands. It seems like you'd have to be horrendously unlucky for it to happen to you.
I also think that it could do with another effect, like progressing to full blown auto-cannibalism. That may be too obvious but I think it needs a little something.

I do like the corrective procedure, forced deafness is a scary concept, especially if you decide it for yourself.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Eh, I don't mind body horror when its used to help the article, but when the article is solely about body horror I tend to find it a cheap trick.

I did find the cannibalization of hands amusing though, so I'll just leave it as a no-vote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I love body horror, and this does that pretty well. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is a good SCP, I saw in the comment session that you showed the idea in the chat.
I should say good work, don't hesitate for doing that again, I upvoted your article.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Bluh. Typo. My apologies.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Neutral vote for me, but I got a question to ask: The last row of table, the year is stated "20111". Is that a typo or on purpose?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The fact that this is a good 1st SCP and that you had minimal feedback makes me want to cancle my vote just so I can upvote again.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I ran it through both chat and forums. I got a bit of feedback and appreciated what feedback I received, but overall the response was underwhelming. I figured I'd just post it already, and come what may.

If it's not your cup of tea, trust me, I understand. I knew the references to popular songs and bands would be particularly divisive, but I decided this concept needed a trial by fire.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Did you run this through chat and/or the forums? It's pretty good if you didn't (Hell, it's not too shabby regardless of the amount of feedback you got), but the number of popular albums this affects and the necessity of surgically induced deafness just kills suspension of disbelief for me. I'm also not too sensitive to body horror, so this just doesn't float my upboat.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Not too shabby. I didn't really want to sleep tonight anyway. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Fixed a few typos for you. Well done. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Oh god first SCP please don't hit me I'm very frail and scared.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'm not entirely sure what to make of it. However, body horror is always a favorite of mine, so for now I'm inclined to look favorably upon this article.
